response.evidence: The data shows substantial disparities in acceptance rates across racial groups. Asian applicants have the highest acceptance rates (86.5-87.8%), followed by White applicants (79.2-84.5%), while Black or African American applicants have notably lower acceptance rates (64.8-73.0%), and American Indian or Alaska Native applicants range from 64.8-75.3%. These differences persist despite comparable average debt-to-income ratios (43-44% across all groups) and similar loan-to-value ratios. For example, Asian females (non-Hispanic) have an 86.5% acceptance rate with avg income of $176K, while Black females (non-Hispanic) have only 73.0% acceptance with similar avg income of $136K and comparable debt ratios. The 13-23 percentage point gaps in acceptance rates between racial groups, with consistent patterns showing lower rates for Black and Native American applicants, suggest potential discrimination.

Acceptance rates vary significantly across demographic groups. Asian applicants show the highest acceptance rates (86.5-87.8% for non-Hispanic), while Black or African American applicants show substantially lower rates (64.8-73.0%). White applicants fall in between (75.9-84.5%). American Indian/Alaska Native groups show rates of 64.8-75.3%. These disparities of over 20 percentage points between highest and lowest performing groups, particularly the consistent pattern where Asian applicants have highest acceptance and Black applicants have lowest acceptance across gender categories, suggest potential discrimination. The financial metrics (loan amounts, income, debt-to-income ratios) show some variation but do not fully explain the magnitude of acceptance rate differences.

The data shows significant disparities in acceptance rates across racial groups. Asian applicants have the highest acceptance rates (86.5-87.8%), followed by White applicants (79.2-84.5%), while Black or African American applicants have notably lower acceptance rates (64.8-73.0%), and American Indian or Alaska Native applicants range from 64.8-75.3%. These disparities persist despite similar average debt-to-income ratios across all groups (43-44%) and comparable loan-to-value ratios. For example, Asian females (non-Hispanic) have an 86.5% acceptance rate with avg income of 177K, while Black females (non-Hispanic) have a 73.0% acceptance rate with similar debt-to-income ratio (43.9% vs 43.7%) but lower avg income of 136K. The consistent pattern of lower acceptance rates for certain racial groups, even when financial metrics are comparable, suggests potential discrimination in the approval process.

Significant disparities in acceptance rates exist across racial groups. Asian applicants show the highest acceptance rates (86.5-87.8%), followed by White applicants (79.2-84.5%), while Black or African American applicants have notably lower acceptance rates (64.8-73.0%), and American Indian or Alaska Native applicants show similar patterns (64.8-75.3%). These disparities persist despite comparable average debt-to-income ratios (43-44% across all groups) and loan-to-value ratios. For example, Asian Female Non-Hispanic applicants have an 86.5% acceptance rate while Black Female Non-Hispanic applicants with similar debt-to-income ratios (43.7% vs 43.9%) have only 73.0% acceptance. The consistency of these patterns across sex and ethnicity subcategories within each race suggests systemic disparities in approval outcomes.

Significant disparities in acceptance rates exist across racial groups with similar loan characteristics. Asian applicants show acceptance rates of 86.5-87.8%, White applicants 75.9-84.5%, while Black or African American applicants show 64.8-73.0% and American Indian or Alaska Native applicants 64.8-75.3%. These disparities persist despite comparable average debt-to-income ratios (43-44% across all groups) and similar loan-to-value ratios. For example, Asian females (non-Hispanic) have 86.5% acceptance with avg loan $522k, while Black females (non-Hispanic) have 73.0% acceptance with lower avg loan $398k and similar financial metrics. The 13-23 percentage point gaps in acceptance rates between demographic groups with comparable financial profiles suggests potential discrimination.

The data shows substantial disparities in acceptance rates across racial groups. Asian applicants have the highest acceptance rates (86.5-87.8%), followed by White applicants (79.2-84.5%), while Black or African American applicants have notably lower acceptance rates (64.8-73.0%), and American Indian or Alaska Native applicants similarly lower rates (64.8-75.3%). These differences of 15-23 percentage points persist despite comparable average debt-to-income ratios across all groups (43-44 range) and similar loan-to-value ratios. Asian and White applicants do show higher average incomes and request larger loan amounts, but Black or African American applicants with similar financial metrics to other groups still face materially lower acceptance rates, suggesting factors beyond standard financial qualifications may be influencing outcomes.

The data shows substantial disparities in acceptance rates across racial groups. Asian applicants have the highest acceptance rates (86.5-87.8%), followed by White applicants (79.2-84.5%), while Black or African American applicants have notably lower acceptance rates (64.8-73.0%), and American Indian or Alaska Native applicants range from 64.8-75.3%. These differences persist despite similar average debt-to-income ratios across groups (all approximately 43-44%) and comparable loan characteristics. For example, Asian Female Non-Hispanic applicants have an 86.5% acceptance rate while Black Female Non-Hispanic applicants have a 73.0% acceptance rate, despite the Black applicants requesting lower average loan amounts ($397,631 vs $522,172). This pattern of lower acceptance rates for certain racial groups with similar or more favorable financial metrics suggests potential discrimination.

Significant disparities in acceptance rates exist across racial groups with similar loan characteristics. Asian applicants show acceptance rates of 86.5-87.8%, White applicants 75.9-84.5%, while Black or African American applicants show 64.8-73.0% and American Indian or Alaska Native applicants 64.8-75.3%. These disparities persist despite comparable average debt-to-income ratios (all groups clustering around 43-44%) and loan-to-value ratios. For example, Asian Female Non-Hispanic applicants have 86.5% acceptance with avg loan $522K, while Black Female Non-Hispanic applicants have 73.0% acceptance with lower avg loan $398K, suggesting they face higher rejection rates despite requesting smaller loans relative to Asians.

Acceptance rates vary significantly across racial groups with similar application profiles. Asian applicants show highest acceptance rates (86.5-87.8% for non-Hispanic), while Black or African American applicants show substantially lower rates (70.5-73.0% for non-Hispanic), despite comparable average debt-to-income ratios (43-44% across all groups) and similar loan characteristics. White non-Hispanic applicants fall in between (83.8-84.5%). The 13-17 percentage point gap in acceptance rates between Asian and Black applicants, given similar financial metrics, suggests potential discrimination in the approval process.

The data shows significant disparities in acceptance rates across racial groups. Asian applicants have the highest acceptance rates (86.5-87.8%), followed by White applicants (79.2-84.5%), while Black or African American applicants have notably lower acceptance rates (64.8-73.0%), and American Indian or Alaska Native applicants range from 64.8-75.3%. These differences persist even though financial metrics (avg_loan_amount, avg_debt_to_income_ratio, avg_income) are relatively similar across groups. For example, Black Male Non-Hispanic applicants have avg_income of 159.6k and acceptance rate of 70.5%, while Asian Male Non-Hispanic applicants with similar debt-to-income ratios (43.4 vs 44.0) have avg_income of 209.5k and acceptance rate of 87.8%. The consistent pattern of lower acceptance rates for certain racial groups, with differences exceeding 15-20 percentage points, suggests potential discrimination that cannot be explained by the financial variables shown.
